Dear Judge mateom
This letter will confirm our telephone conversation of January in
outlining the answers to questions you presented in your letters
to the commission dated December 17 and December 21 concerning items
which must be reported by you on your statement of Economic Interests.
You: letter of December 17 asked the foilowinq questions:
1.
Are you required to report an interest in real property
located in Riverside County?
Yes.
The Jurisdiction of a superior court Judge is the
State of California and, therefore, any real property
held within the state must be reported.
2.
Are you required to report the income received from an
irrevocable trust of which you are a trustee and
beneficiary?
You are required to report the investments, any other
holdings and any income of the trust which would otherwise
he reportable under the law.
at
Are you required to report stocks received from a dividend
4.
reinveetrent program, the monetary value of which is less
than 5150?
Are the shares received in this manner reportable?
no.
You are only required to report investments of $1,000
or more.
Dividends received from stocks registered with the
Securities and Exchange commission are not reportehle as
incomei

no.
You’ letter of December 21 asks whether or not you must report
gifts including an eight-day excursion on a chartered heat received
from a pcbential employer or your husband.
All gifts received by
111 elected official with a value of $25 or more rust he reported.
You must estimate the value of the gifts received if the actual
Value cannot be ascertained.
you need only report the gifts
received by you and not those {nits received by your husband.

I am a Judge of the Superior Court or Los Angeles
County.
(1)
Am I required to report an interest in real property
iocated in Riverside County?
I am a trustee and beneficiary on an irrevocable inter—
vivos trust estahiished many years ago by my parents as a
girt.
(2)
Am I required to report the income received from this
trust?
Am I required to list es an asset my proportionate
interest in the trust?
I am a trustee of a testamentary trust in which I have
no legal or beneficial interest.
(3)
Am I required to report as assets an or the corpus
of the trust (stocks) standing in my name as trustee?
I own two stocks in which I have a dividend reinvest—
ment program, i.e., instead of receiving the dividends
I receive shares end/or fractional shares, the monetary
Value of which is less than $150.
(A)
Is the acquisition or the shares in this manner and
this amount required to be reported?
I have designed to en eduit non-dependent child as
custodian tor a minor, dependent chiid, pursuant to the
Uniform nuts to Minors Act, the rental ($75 per month)
received rrom an occupant of a garage apartmenti
Neither
I nor the minor child have access to this income.
(5)
Am I required to report this income?
My husband is an elected county otriciai.
In August
a comitee acting on his behalf heid s fund-raising event.
He and the committee are required by iew to file a cam—
paign statement with the county Registrar~Recurder pur-
suant to Government Code
§§ Bbzoo— uzih, with
which they
have compiied.
(6)
Am I required to report the proceeds of this event?
If so, may I do so by rererence to the statement on file?

Gentlemen:
In addition to the questions previously
submitted by me under date of December 17, 1975,
I am inquiring further as per the enclosed.

wancy a. Watson‘
.
During 1976 my husband entered into a series of negotia—
tions with the chairman or the Board and majority stockholder
of a nationwide corporation to assume the position of
President of that corporation.
The corporation is head—
quartered in San Francisco, California, and has producing
facilities in seven cities in the united States from coast
to coast.
The negotiations were initiated by a phone call
go? the Chairman of the Board of the corporation on May 5,
My husband flew to San Francisco on May 1”,
1976, and
discussed the ofrer with the chairman of the board and a
member of the Board or Directors.
The neuter of the Board
of Directors is also president of a related company which
does business in the western United states.
During these
meetings, a series of oral agreements were reached with
respect to salary, stock options and certain fringe benefits.
Agreement was also reach that Irw husband would assume the
duties of President of the corporation on March 1, 1977.
The matter was then placed in the hands of attorneys for the
drafting of an agreement and negotiations continued through
the summer to finalize a contract.
The Chairman of the
Board invited my husband and me to Join him, along with the
Prelident of the related company and member of the board or
Directors and two attorneys who are outside counsel for the
corporate comlexes and the house counsel for the corporation,
on a trip.
We joined them on a chartered boat trip out of
Vancouver from September to to September 12, 1976, ror the
purpose of finalizing some agreement with respect to m
husband's joining the corporation.
My husband and I flew to Vancouver on September 3 and
returned from Seattle on September 12, 1976.
All expenses
were paid by the Chairman of the Board.
Negotiations broke
down and a decision was reached by both sides to terminate
further negotiations.
within two weeks following September
12, 1976, another person was named president or that corpora-
tion.
The questions is, are the expenses for the trip report.
able under the Fair political practices Act?
If so, how and
in what manner should they be treated?

sections.conclusion: Real property in California must be reported regardless of county location. Trust income and holdings are reportable if otherwise reportable under law. Investments of $1,000 or more must be reported; dividend reinvestment shares valued under $150 need not be reported. Income from trusts where the judge has no access is not reportable. Campaign funds for a spouse need not be reported by the judge. All gifts of $25 or more must be reported, including the eight-day excursion; the judge need only report gifts received by herself, not those received by her husband.
sections.question_synthetic: Judge Nancy E. Watson seeks guidance on multiple reporting requirements under the Fair Political Practices Act regarding real property interests, trust assets and income, dividend reinvestment programs, income from trusts for minor children, campaign fundraising proceeds for her husband, and gifts including a chartered boat trip from a potential employer.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC advises that Judge Watson must report California real property and reportable trust assets/income, but need not report small dividend reinvestments, inaccessible trust income, or spouse's campaign funds. All gifts valued at $25 or more must be reported, including the estimated value of the chartered boat trip, though only gifts received personally by the judge (not her husband) require reporting.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document includes incoming requests dated December 17 and December 21, 1976, followed by FPPC response dated February 22, 1977. OCR quality is moderate with some scanning artifacts. All six questions from December 17 letter and the gift question from December 21 letter are addressed in the response. Response is signed by Regina Siciliano-Kutchins, Technical Assistance Program Coordinator.

CONCLUSION: Real property in California must be reported regardless of county location. Trust income and holdings are reportable if otherwise reportable under law. Investments of $1,000 or more must be reported; dividend reinvestment shares valued under $150 need not be reported. Income from trusts where the judge has no access is not reportable. Campaign funds for a spouse need not be reported by the judge. All gifts of $25 or more must be reported, including the eight-day excursion; the judge need only report gifts received by herself, not those received by her husband.

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to Judge Nancy E. Watson's inquiries regarding economic interest disclosure requirements, providing detailed guidance on reporting obligations for real property, trusts, dividend programs, campaign funds, and gifts received from a potential employer.
